Title: Opinion: Tax system biggest barrier to unlocking Saskatchewan's potential
Author: Steve Bartlett
Summary: Saskatchewan’s fiscal outlook is bleak, with deficits expected to continue into 2026, and debt nearly tripling since 2007. The province faces high public sector growth, stagnant private sector wages, and rising social issues. Premier Scott Moe warns of ongoing financial challenges, emphasizing the need for economic growth and tax reform rather than just cuts to stabilize finances.
Content:
Ahead of this month’s provincial budget, Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe has warned that Finance Minister Jim Reiter won’t be wearing his black shoes. After promising a balanced budget in 2025, Saskatchewan’s books are set to finish the year deep in the red, and remain there in 2026. 

According to the mid-year report, Saskatchewan’s total net debt is on track to have nearly tripled since fiscal 2007, rising as a share of the economy by more than three per cent. The provincial opposition has been quick to lay the blame for deficits on the Moe administration’s “waste” and “mismanagement.” They maintain that cuts alone can right the fiscal ship, and that Saskatchewan doesn’t have a “revenue problem.” 

On the surface, Saskatchewan’s spending does need better control. Expenses have far outpaced inflation and population growth. If Saskatchewan had held expenses to inflation and population growth since 2018, the province would have $1.6 billion in additional fiscal capacity this year. 

Saskatchewan’s public sector has skyrocketed relative to the overall economy. In 2025, public sector employment reached its highest share of total employment in at least 50 years. The public sector is now a larger proportion of Saskatchewan’s economy than it was when parts of the oil and potash sectors were government-owned. 

At the same time, Saskatchewan has Canada’s highest rates of child poverty and the highest rates of crime. Nearly a quarter of the province lacks access to a family doctor, and education funding faces a nearly $500 million shortfall from 2013 levels. These are not issues that can be addressed solely through cuts. 

The rising share of the public sector as a percentage of the economy is not driven by excessive growth in government employment but by a lack of growth in the private sector. Since 2014, real private sector per capita GDP has fallen by nearly four per cent in Saskatchewan, compared to a one per cent increase at the national level. Private sector employment grew nearly five times slower than the national average. Sixty-seven per cent of new jobs nationwide since 2014 were created in the private sector, while in Saskatchewan, 72 per cent of new jobs were in the public sector. 

The reality is that Saskatchewan’s economy is not growing fast enough to support stable funding for high-quality services. While some spending areas need to be cut, the complete solution to Saskatchewan’s fiscal decline lies in unleashing growth potential. The province’s tax system is the biggest barrier to this, as Saskatchewan directly taxes job creation and investment through its PST on business inputs, machinery and equipment, building materials, and construction labour—costs that few other provinces tax.

Title: Israel dropped 'propaganda' leaflets in Lebanon amid ongoing offensive against Hezbollah
Summary: Israel dropped propaganda leaflets over Beirut amid its offensive against Hezbollah, warning residents to disarm and share intelligence, with QR codes linking to chats. Experts suggest the leaflets could constitute a war crime, citing previous similar actions in Gaza. The conflict has resulted in over 600 deaths and 800,000 displaced in Lebanon. Residents found the leaflets and loud booms frightening.
Content:
Israel dropped propaganda leaflets in Lebanon amid ongoing offensive against Hezbollah

On Friday, two loud booms echoed in Beirut before Israeli leaflets started raining down on residents, spreading captions that evoke Israel's ongoing offensive in Gaza and urge Lebanese residents to share information with Israeli intelligence. Experts explain why they amount to propaganda and possibly a war crime.

One expert said the leaflets could amount to a war crime, while another blames the conflict on Hezbollah.

After widening strikes across Lebanon, Israel's military dropped leaflets warning people to disarm Hezbollah or face the consequences. Lebanon reports that the fighting has killed more than 600 people and displaced 800,000 others.

The leaflets included captions that evoke Israel's ongoing offensive in Gaza and urged Lebanese residents to share information with Israeli intelligence. In light of the success in Gaza, the leaflets stated, "The New Reality Newspaper arrives in Lebanon." They appealed directly to Lebanese citizens, encouraging them to contact Unit 504, an intelligence arm of the Israel Defence Forces (IDF), if they "want to be part of real change and contribute to the prosperity and protection of your country." QR codes on the leaflets directed people to chats on Facebook and WhatsApp, but Lebanese authorities and residents warned each other not to scan the codes out of fear of data breaches.

Other statements on the leaflets included: "Lebanon is your decision" and "Your land is yours, don't let it become 'free money' for Iranian Hezbollah's weapons."

This is not the first time Israel has dropped leaflets over residential areas. In Gaza, the Israeli army has previously dropped leaflets ordering civilians to evacuate ahead of imminent strikes—a move condemned by human rights group Amnesty International for what they say amounts to forced displacement of civilians and collective punishment, both considered war crimes. Israel has repeatedly stated that such leaflets are meant to protect civilians by warning them of strikes in advance.

A leaflet dropped into Beirut on Friday startled residents. Beirut resident Hala Beydoun described the moment as "very scary." "First there were these big, strong booms. We thought it was a rocket or a drone," she told CBC News. "Then, we saw leaflets raining from the skies, like, what the heck?"

Title: Russian strike on Kyiv region kills 4, wounds 15
Author: Unknown
Summary: A Russian missile and drone attack on Kyiv region killed at least four people and wounded 15, damaging infrastructure and targeting energy facilities. Ukraine reported around 430 drones and 68 missiles used. Russia claimed the strikes targeted military and industrial sites. The U.S. postponed talks with Russia amid Middle East tensions, while Zelenskyy urged increased Western air defense support.
Content:
A combined Russian missile and drone attack on the Kyiv region killed at least four people and wounded 15, according to a Ukrainian official. The attack affected four districts, damaging residential buildings, educational institutions, enterprises, and critical infrastructure. The head of the regional administration, Mykola Kalashnyk, stated that three of the wounded were in critical condition.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said the main target of the strikes was the energy infrastructure of the Kyiv region. He reported that Russia launched around 430 drones and 68 missiles. Russia's Defense Ministry stated that the nighttime strikes targeted energy and industrial facilities serving Ukraine's armed forces, as well as military airfields.

The United States postponed planned talks with Russia due to the ongoing war in the Middle East. The conflict has led to increased global energy prices and has been seen by Russia as an opportunity to benefit from the turmoil. Zelenskyy called on Western partners to increase the production of air defense missiles amid the attacks.

Title: Former NATO chief says he doesn't think allies will be pulled further into Middle East conflict
Summary: NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg stated he does not believe allies will become further involved in the Middle East conflict, but warned Iran's war could benefit Russia’s economy and impact global stability. He emphasized NATO's limited role and highlighted concerns over rising energy prices, inflation, and global economic consequences amid the Iran-Israel-U.S. conflict, which has included the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
Content:
The former secretary general of NATO, Jens Stoltenberg, has stated that he does not believe allies will be pulled further into the conflict in the Middle East. However, he expressed concern that the ongoing war with Iran could benefit Russia's economy and divert global attention from the invasion of Ukraine.

Stoltenberg emphasized that NATO has not played a significant role in Middle East conflicts and stressed the importance of preventing NATO from becoming involved. In an interview on CBC's The House, he noted, "I think it's important to prevent NATO [from becoming] involved in the conflict."

While he does not foresee NATO allies becoming further entangled in the war, which began two weeks ago with Israel and the United States launching air attacks across Iran, Stoltenberg warned of the broader global impacts. "The economic consequences are significant. Increased energy prices can lead to reduced global growth and increased inflation," he said. "It's a combination of the human suffering with the economic consequences which is making me, of course, concerned."

The conflict has led to the shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz for ten days, causing global unease about an oil crisis. Iranian authorities report more than 1,300 deaths, while Israel has reported 12 fatalities. The U.S. has lost at least 11 soldiers, with eight more severely injured. Iran's government has retaliated by closing the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial passage for global oil shipments, causing oil prices to rise as high as $100 per barrel.

Title: Iran War Live Updates: U.S. Tells Citizens to Leave Iraq After Second Attack on Embassy
Author: The New York Times
Summary: The U.S. Embassy in Baghdad urged Americans to leave Iraq immediately after being attacked by Iran-backed militias, notably Kataib Hezbollah. U.S. airstrikes targeted Iran’s Kharg Island. Tensions escalated with Israel bombing Tehran and Beirut, while Iran launched missile and drone attacks on Israel. Civilians in Iran and Lebanon suffered heavy casualties amid ongoing regional conflict.
Content:
The U.S. Embassy in Baghdad urged all American citizens to leave Iraq immediately on Saturday after the embassy was attacked overnight for the second time since the war with Iran started. The warning said militias allied with Iran had carried out numerous attacks on targets associated with the United States, including diplomatic facilities, American companies, and hotels frequented by foreigners. It recommended Americans travel overland to neighboring countries because commercial flights were not operating and warned them not to come to the embassy or a U.S. consulate in the northern city of Erbil.

Kataib Hezbollah, one of several Iran-backed militias in Iraq, claimed responsibility for the attack on Saturday, saying it had fired on the embassy the previous night. A video verified by The New York Times showed a structure on the embassy’s roof was on fire. Two Iraqi security officials who were not authorized to speak publicly confirmed the attack but could not give additional details.

Overnight, U.S. airstrikes hit military targets on Kharg Island, home to Iran’s main oil export terminal. President Trump threatened to bomb Iranian oil infrastructure on the island if Tehran did not allow ships to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow opening to the Persian Gulf through which a fifth of the world’s oil must transit. But Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps defied Mr. Trump’s threat, asserting in a statement that the critical waterway was under its full control and that “any attempt to move or transit will be targeted.” Mr. Trump also called on other countries, including Britain, France, and China, to send warships to Iran to try to ensure the oil tankers could pass through the strait, promising the United States would coordinate with them. Oil prices have risen steeply since the United States and Israel launched their air war on Iran two weeks ago, rattling world markets.

Israel’s military bombarded the Iranian capital of Tehran on Saturday and the southern outskirts of the Lebanese capital of Beirut, while Hezbollah, the Iran-backed armed group, fired rockets and drones into Israeli territory. A heavy, lifeless atmosphere hung over the streets of Tehran on Saturday, residents said. “No one knows what will happen,” said Meisam, 41, a resident and poet who asked that his last name not be used for fear of reprisal. “And we are all somewhere on the edge between depression and hope.”

At least 1,348 civilians in Iran have been killed since the start of the war, Iran’s representative to the United Nations told the Security Council on Wednesday, the latest figure the country has provided. In Lebanon, officials said that 826 people had been killed and over 2,000 others injured.

Officials in several Persian Gulf nations reported a wave of aerial attacks overnight as Iran continued to escalate its military actions.

Title: Netanyahu Has the War He Always Wanted, but on Trump’s Terms
Author: David M. Halbfinger
Summary: Prime Minister Netanyahu initially aimed to eliminate Iran’s threat but now focuses on degrading its nuclear and missile capabilities. Israel targets Iran’s security forces, hoping for internal regime change, but faces limits. The U.S. influences Israeli strikes, restricting their scope, as American officials express concern over damaging Iran’s infrastructure.
Content:
Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu went to war on Feb. 28, promising that Israel and the United States would “remove the existential threat posed by the terrorist regime in Iran.” Two weeks later, though Israeli and U.S. fighters own the skies over Iran, Mr. Netanyahu’s war aims have been brought sharply down to earth. Israel, he says now, is merely to degrade the Islamic Republic as a nuclear and ballistic missile threat.

As for regime change, Israel continues to target Iran’s internal security forces, Mr. Netanyahu said on Thursday in his first news conference of the war. Beyond that, it can do little more than hope for the best—that the Iranian people find a way to overthrow their government someday, perhaps even soon.

It is a striking shift, pointing not only to the difficulty of trying to achieve challenging political objectives with air power alone, but also to an irony of the Israeli leader’s position. More than 30 years after he first publicly identified Iran and its nuclear ambitions as a singular menace to his country, Mr. Netanyahu has finally gotten the war he always wanted when the United States joined the fight shoulder to shoulder with Israel.

But the war is being fought on President Trump’s terms. The United States is exerting influence over what Israel may destroy in Iran. It demonstrated that a week ago when American politicians expressed displeasure with Israel for attacking fuel-storage depots in and around Tehran, filling the skies with large clouds of black smoke.

“Please be cautious about what targets you select,” Senator Lindsey Graham, a Republican of South Carolina and a Trump ally, wrote on social media the next day, stressing the need to preserve such infrastructure to give the Iranian people a chance at a functioning economy, should they manage to overthrow their government.

Title: Moving From Moment.js To The JS Temporal API
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Joe Attardi)
Summary: JavaScript's handling of time has evolved from the basic Date API to libraries like Moment.js, which added features but had issues like size and mutability. Moment is now in maintenance mode. The latest development is the Temporal API, integrated into ECMAScript and expected to become standard by March 2026, offering a modern solution for date and time management.
Content:
The way JavaScript handles time has evolved significantly, from the built-in Date API to Moment.js and now Temporal. The new standard fills gaps in the original Date API while addressing limitations found in Moment and other libraries. 

Almost any kind of application written in JavaScript works with times or dates in some capacity. In the beginning, this was limited to the built-in Date API. This API includes basic functionality, but is quite limited in what it can do. Third-party libraries like Moment.js, and later built-in APIs such as the Intl APIs and the new Temporal API, add much greater flexibility to working with times and dates.

Moment.js is a JavaScript library with powerful utilities for working with times and dates. It includes missing features from the basic Date API, such as time zone manipulation, and makes many common operations simpler. Moment also includes functions for formatting dates and times. It became a widely used library in many different applications.

However, Moment also had its share of issues. It’s a large library, and can add significantly to an application’s bundle size. Because the library doesn’t support tree shaking (a feature of modern bundlers that can remove unused parts of libraries), the entire Moment library is included even if you only use one or two of its functions.

Another issue with Moment is the fact that the objects it creates are mutable. Calling certain functions on a Moment object has side effects and mutates the value of that object. This can lead to unexpected behavior or bugs.

In 2020, the maintainers of Moment decided to put the library into maintenance mode. No new feature development is being done, and the maintainers recommend against using it for new projects.

There are other JavaScript date libraries, such as date-fns, but there’s a new player in town: an API built directly into JavaScript—Temporal. It’s a new standard that fills in the holes of the original Date API as well as solves some of the limitations found in Moment and other libraries.

Temporal is a new time and date API being added to the ECMAScript standard, which defines modern JavaScript. As of March 2026, it has reached Stage 4 of the standardization process.

Title: Beyond `border-radius`: What The CSS `corner-shape` Property Unlocks For Everyday UI
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Brecht De Ruyte)
Summary: The new CSS `corner-shape` property enhances border-radius by allowing diverse corner styles such as bevel, scoop, squircle, notch, and square, addressing long-standing limitations and hacks. It works alongside border-radius, enabling customizable shapes per corner. This feature simplifies complex designs, reduces brittle workarounds, and is expected to gradually be adopted across browsers.
Content:
For years, developers have been hacking around the limitations of border-radius, using clip-path, SVG masks, and fragile workarounds just to get anything other than round corners. The new corner-shape property finally changes that, opening the door to beveled, scooped, and squircle corners.

When I first started building websites, rounded corners required five background images, one for each corner, one for the body, and a prayer that the client wouldn’t ask for a different radius. Then the border-radius property landed, and the entire web collectively sighed with relief. That was over fifteen years ago, and honestly, we’ve been riding that same wave ever since. Just as then, I hope that we can look at this feature as a progressive enhancement slowly making its way to other browsers.

I like a good border-radius like any other person, but the fact is that it only gives us one shape: round. That’s it. Want beveled corners? Clip-path. Scooped ticket edges? SVG mask. Squircle app icons? A carefully tuned SVG that you hope nobody asks you to animate. We’ve been hacking around the limitations of border-radius for years, and those hacks come with real trade-offs: borders don’t follow clip-paths, shadows get cut off, and you end up with brittle code that breaks the moment someone changes a padding value.

Well, the new corner-shape changes all of that.

What Is corner-shape?

The corner-shape property is a companion to border-radius. It doesn’t replace it; it modifies the shape of the curve that border-radius creates. Without border-radius, corner-shape does nothing. But together, they’re a powerful pair.

The property accepts these values: round (the default, same as regular border-radius), squircle (a superellipse, the smooth Apple-style rounded square), bevel (a straight line between the two radius endpoints, creating snipped corners), scoop (an inverted curve, creating concave corners), notch (sharp inward cuts), and square (effectively removing the rounding, overriding border-radius).

You can set different values per corner, just like border-radius:

```css
/* top-left, top-right, bottom-right, bottom-left */
.element {
  border-radius: 25px;
  corner-shape: superellipse(0); /* equal to 'bevel' */
}
```

So the question here might be: why not call this property “border-shape” instead?

Title: Building Dynamic Forms In React And Next.js
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Sunil Sandhu)
Summary: The article discusses the limitations of using React Hook Form, Zod, and React Query for complex forms with conditional logic, which can turn forms into decision processes. It introduces an alternative approach using SurveyJS, treating forms as data (JSON schema) rather than component trees. The piece compares both methods through building a four-step form, highlighting when to choose each model.
Content:
There’s a mental model most React developers share without ever discussing it out loud. That forms are always supposed to be components. This means a stack like:

React Hook Form for local state (minimal re-renders, ergonomic field registration, imperative interaction).

Zod for validation (input correctness, boundary validation, type-safe parsing).

React Query for backend: submission, retries, caching, server sync, and so on.

And for the vast majority of forms — your login screens, your settings pages, your CRUD modals — this works really well. Each piece does its job, they compose cleanly, and you can move on to the parts of your application that actually differentiate your product.

But every once in a while, a form starts accumulating things like visibility rules that depend on earlier answers, or derived values that cascade through three fields. Maybe even entire pages that should be skipped or shown based on a running total.

You handle the first conditional with a useWatch and an inline branch, which is fine. Then another. Then you’re reaching for superRefine to encode cross-field rules that your Zod schema can’t express in the normal way. Then, step navigation starts leaking business logic. At some point, you look at what you’ve built and realize that the form isn’t really UI anymore. It’s more of a decision process, and the component tree is just where you happened to store it.

This is where I think the mental model for forms in React breaks down, and it’s really nobody’s fault. The RHF + Zod stack is excellent at what it was designed for.

The issue is that we tend to keep using it past the point where its abstractions match the problem because the alternative requires a different way of thinking about forms entirely.

This article is about that alternative. To show this, we’ll build the exact same multi-step form twice:

With React Hook Form + Zod wired to React Query for submission,

With Survey JS, which treats a form as data — a simple JSON schema — rather than a component tree.

Same requirements, same conditional logic, same API call at the end. Then we’ll map exactly what moved and what stayed, and lay out a practical way to decide which model you should use, and when.

The form we’re building:

This form will use a 4-step flow:

Title: Laravel Pint Now Replaces Fully Qualified Class Names with Imports
Author: Paul Redmond
Summary: Laravel Pint's preset now includes the fully_qualified_strict_types rule, which converts FQCNs to short class names with use statements in code and PHPDoc annotations. When run, it updates FQCNs across the codebase, adding necessary imports. The rule is active by default in Pint's Laravel preset; custom presets can enable it via configuration. Changes are cosmetic and do not affect runtime.

Title: Laracon AU Returns to Brisbane - Call for Speakers Now Open
Author: Michael Dyrynda
Summary: Laracon AU will be held in Brisbane, Australia, from November 4-6, 2026, focusing on deep technical Laravel topics. The Call for Papers is open until April 17, inviting submissions on practical architecture, patterns, and real-world solutions. The event aims to connect Laravel developers from the APAC region and beyond, with additional pre-conference activities planned.
Content:
Laracon AU will return to Brisbane, Australia on November 4-6, 2026, bringing the Laravel community together once again. Now in its sixth year, Laracon AU has become a gathering point for Laravel developers across the APAC region and beyond. Attendees can expect two days of technical talks, hallway conversations, and shared experiences building real applications with Laravel.

The Call for Papers is now open, inviting the community to submit talks for this year's event. The 2026 schedule will place a stronger emphasis on deep technical content and real-world implementation. Talks will focus on practical architecture, patterns used in production, and lessons learned from solving complex problems in modern Laravel apps. Topics may include scaling queues, designing resilient architecture, performance tuning, testing strategies, or integrating emerging tooling into Laravel workflows. The goal is to surface talks that developers can immediately apply to their own projects. Those who have built something interesting, solved difficult problems, or discovered beneficial patterns are encouraged to submit.

Laracon AU 2026 will take place in Brisbane, a city known for its warm spring weather, riverfront precincts, and vibrant developer community. Attendees traveling from interstate or overseas may want to plan their arrival earlier in the week. There will be additional opportunities to learn, connect, and dive deeper into Laravel before the main conference begins, with activities planned for Wednesday. It is recommended to arrive Tuesday evening or early Wednesday and depart after 8 pm on Friday to fully enjoy the event.

The conference continues to bring together developers from across the Laravel ecosystem—including engineers, maintainers, founders, and community members—to share ideas and advance the craft. Updates regarding speaker announcements, ticket releases, and additional event details will be shared in the coming months. 

The Call for Papers closes on April 17th. Interested speakers are encouraged to submit their proposals now.

Title: 2nd Homicide of the year
Author: Unknown
Summary: Saskatoon police are investigating a homicide on 22nd Street West on March 14, 2026, after a man was shot and died from his injuries. The Major Crime Section is leading the investigation. Police ask for any information to be directed to 306-975-8300 or Crime Stoppers at 1-800-222-8477.
Content: Saskatoon Police are investigating a homicide that occurred in the 900 block of 22nd Street West around 12:30 a.m. on March 14, 2026. Officers responded to a shooting and found a male suffering from a gunshot wound. He was taken to hospital but later died from his injuries. The Saskatoon Police Service Major Crime Section is leading the investigation. Anyone with information is asked to contact the Saskatoon Police Service at 306-975-8300 or Crime Stoppers at 1-800-222-8477.

Title: Arrest - Assault / Weapon - 1200 Block Avenue H North
Author: Unknown
Summary: A man was arrested on March 8, 2026, after an assault report at a residence on Avenue H North. Officers found him attempting to destroy drugs and ammunition. A search warrant recovered a handgun, prohibited magazine, ammunition, and holster. The 35-year-old faces six firearms charges and assault causing bodily harm.
Content:
One man was arrested following a report of an assault. Shortly before 3:00 a.m. on March 8, 2026, members of patrol were called to a residence in the 1200 block of Avenue H North for a report of an assault. Upon arrival, officers entered the residence and located the suspect attempting to destroy illicit drugs and ammunition. He was taken into custody, and members of the Guns and Gangs Unit were notified to assist with the investigation. 

Following the execution of a search warrant, the following items were seized: a KAHR CW9 9mm handgun, a prohibited magazine, ammunition, and a concealable holster. The 35-year-old man is facing six firearms-related charges in addition to assault causing bodily harm.

Title: Arrests - Firearms Investigation - 1300 Block 8th Street East
Author: Unknown
Summary: Two individuals, a 26-year-old male and a 19-year-old female, face 17 charges after police responded to a report of a firearm in a taxi in the 1300 block of 8th Street East. Police seized a pellet gun, bear spray, batons, knives, a machete, cell phones, cash, and methamphetamine. They arrested the pair at a motel.
Content:
Two people are facing charges after police responded to a report of a firearm yesterday. At approximately 9:30 p.m. on March 10, 2026, it was reported that two individuals had left a firearm in the back of a taxi they had exited in the 1300 block of 8th Street East. Police seized the firearm, which was determined to be a pellet gun, and attended a suite in a motel where they arrested a male and female. 

Upon their arrest and further investigation, police located three cans of bear spray, two expandable batons, two knives, a machete, multiple cell phones, cash, and methamphetamine packaged for distribution. A 26-year-old male and a 19-year-old female are facing a total of 17 charges.

Title: Three more Iran football team members change minds over asylum
Author: Unknown
Summary: Three Iranian women's football delegation members granted Australian visas have returned to Iran, reversing their decision to stay in Australia. Initially, five players sought asylum after protesting during the Asian Cup, but only three remain in Australia. The Iranian sports ministry praised the team’s patriotism, while Australian officials confirmed the women’s return, citing genuine choices and interactions with authorities.
Content:
Three more members of the Iranian women's football delegation who were granted humanitarian visas to stay in Australia have changed their minds and will return home. The trio have been named by human rights activists in the Iranian diaspora as Zahra Soltan Meshkehkar, Mona Hamoudi, and Zahra Sarbali.

Concerns grew for the Iranian team after they remained silent during the country's anthem in their opening Asian Cup match against South Korea on 2 March, leading to accusations of being "war traitors" in Iran. Australia's home affairs minister confirmed that his government had done everything possible to ensure the women had a safe future in the country.

A member of the Iranian football delegation granted Australian asylum later decided to change her mind. Five Iranian footballers initially granted visas after their anthem protest have now returned to Iran. Australian officials stated that these women experienced genuine choices in Australia and interacted with authorities to help them.

Iran's sports ministry confirmed the news, first reported by Tasnim News Agency linked to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, stating that "the national spirit and patriotism of the Iranian women's national football team defeated the enemy's plans." The statement also accused Australia's government of "playing in Trump's field." Tasnim reported that the three women were on their way to Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, to rejoin the rest of the squad and were "returning to the warm embrace of their families and homeland after withdrawing their asylum application in Australia." They reportedly resisted "psychological warfare, extensive propaganda, and seductive offers."

Of the seven players who initially expressed a desire to stay in Australia, only three remain as defectors. One player made the decision to return to Iran on Wednesday. Hamoudi and Sarbali were among the original five who refused asylum after slipping away from the team's hotel on the Gold Coast and being taken to a safe house by Australian Federal Police. Zahra Soltan Meshkehkar, a member of the team's technical staff, was among the two women to seek asylum the following day. The other, Mohaddeseh Zolfi, changed her mind hours after being granted the right to stay and has reportedly rejoined the team.

There was concern in Australia that team members and their families might face repercussions in Iran after the players refused to sing the national anthem.

Title: Hamas urges key ally Iran to halt attacks on Gulf states
Author: Unknown
Summary: Hamas called on Iran to cease attacks on Gulf states, urging regional cooperation, while affirming Iran's right to defend itself against US and Israeli strikes. Iran's retaliatory missile and drone attacks have killed at least 18 people across the Gulf. Hamas, part of Iran’s "axis of resistance," expressed concern over regional war and called for an immediate halt.
Content:
The Palestinian armed group Hamas has called on Iran to stop attacking Gulf states, in a rare appeal to its key ally. The Tehran-backed group urged its "brothers in Iran to avoid targeting neighbouring countries," stating that all regional nations should cooperate "to preserve the bonds of brotherhood." At the same time, Hamas, which runs Gaza, affirmed Tehran's right to defend itself against attacks by the US and Israel, which are continuing to strike Iran.

Iranian authorities have said their retaliatory strikes target "American installations" on Gulf soil rather than the neighbors themselves, but many attacks have hit civilian infrastructure. Hamas said on Saturday that it had been following the war in the region with "deep concern" and called upon "all states and international organisations to work towards halting [the war] immediately." Iran has not publicly commented on Hamas's statement.

Tehran's retaliatory drone and missile strikes have impacted many of its Gulf neighbors over the past two weeks. At least 18 people have been killed across the region so far, most of them security personnel or foreign workers. Six people have been killed in the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and another six in Kuwait, while Oman, Saudi Arabia, and Bahrain have all reported two deaths each.

The Palestinian group has previously condemned as a "heinous crime" the assassination of Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and joint US-Israeli strikes on the first day of the war on 28 February. Hamas is part of Iran's "axis of resistance," a coalition aimed at countering US and Israeli influence. Turkey and Qatar, which have recently come under Iranian attacks, also provide significant financial and political support to Hamas.

The group fought a devastating war against Israel in Gaza for more than two years. A US-imposed ceasefire began last October, and negotiations continue over subsequent phases of the deal. Israel and Hamas have accused each other of near-daily violations of the ceasefire. The Hamas-run health ministry reported that 649 people had been killed in Gaza since the ceasefire took effect.

Title: More US Marines and warships being moved to Middle East, reports say
Author: Unknown
Summary: US Marines and warships, including the USS Tripoli-led amphibious group, are being deployed to the Middle East amid heightened tensions with Iran. President Trump claimed US forces destroyed Iranian military infrastructure, and Iran threatened to block the Strait of Hormuz. The US is relocating missile defense systems and preparing to escort tankers, with escalated military actions and warnings from Tehran.
Content:
More US Marines and warships are being deployed to the Middle East, two officials confirmed to CBS News, the BBC's US partner. The officials said the reinforcements were to come from an amphibious ready group and its Marine expeditionary unit, with one official adding that the group would be led by the Japan-based USS Tripoli, an amphibious assault ship. The unit headed by the USS Tripoli typically consists of around 5,000 sailors and Marines distributed across several warships.

The development comes as President Donald Trump said US forces had "totally obliterated" Iranian military infrastructure on Kharg Island in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway for global oil shipping. Retaliatory Iranian strikes targeting Israel and US military bases across the Middle East have disrupted major hubs for international air travel and caused the price of oil to soar.

Asked when the US Navy would start escorting tankers through the Strait of Hormuz, Trump told reporters on Friday: "It will happen soon." The Wall Street Journal, which first reported the movement of US personnel, also reported that the request for reinforcements was made by US Central Command, the part of the US military responsible for the Middle East, and approved by Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth.

The US is also relocating parts of a missile defense system installed in South Korea to the Middle East, according to officials cited by the Washington Post and South Korean news outlets. Trump earlier said Iran would be hit "very hard" over the coming week, adding that the war with Iran would be over when "I feel it in my bones." Hegseth also said the US military would show "no mercy for our enemies."

The Pentagon has said it had already struck 6,000 Iranian targets during the first two weeks of conflict, which began when the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran on 28 February, killing Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. Iran's new supreme leader—and son of the former leader—56-year-old Mojtaba Khamenei, said Tehran would continue to block the Strait of Hormuz in his first public address on Thursday. In a statement read out by a newsreader on Iranian state TV, he vowed to "avenge the blood" of Iranians killed in the war with the US and Israel, and warned neighboring countries to stop hosting US bases.

Title: The MacBook Neo is ‘the most repairable MacBook’ in years, according to iFixit
Author: Anthony Ha
Summary: i Fixit praised the Mac Book Neo as the most repairable in years, highlighting its screw-secured battery tray, easier disassembly, and compatibility with Repair Assistant. Despite soldered RAM and storage limiting repairs, the device received a 6/10 repairability score. The Neo is also more affordable, making it a notable upgrade.
Content:
The Mac Book Neo is considered the most repairable Mac Book in years, according to an in-depth teardown by i Fixit. The new model is also notably more affordable. The key improvement highlighted by i Fixit is the battery design; unlike older Mac Books where batteries are glued in place, the Neo’s battery is held by a tray secured with 18 screws. While this is more labor-intensive, i Fixit emphasized that screws are preferable to adhesive, and the new arrangement should make battery replacement significantly easier. 

Other features contributing to its repairability include a flat disassembly tree, compatibility with Repair Assistant for replacement parts, and easier access to the display and keyboard. However, i Fixit expressed some concerns, such as soldered RAM and storage, which limit repair options. Despite these issues, the repairability score given was 6 out of 10, which is considered strong for a Mac Book.

Title: US Army announces contract with Anduril worth up to $20B
Author: Anthony Ha
Summary: The U.S. Army signed a 10-year, up to $20 billion contract with defense startup Anduril for hardware, software, and services, consolidating over 120 procurement actions. The deal emphasizes rapid deployment of software capabilities on the modern battlefield. Anduril, co-founded by Palmer Luckey, is valued at around $60 billion and is known for autonomous military tech.
Content:
The U.S. Army announced late Friday that it has signed a 10-year contract with defense tech startup Anduril. The deal could be worth up to $20 billion. The contract begins with a five-year “base period,” with an option to extend for an additional five years, and includes Anduril hardware, software, infrastructure, and services.

The Army describes the agreement as a single enterprise contract consolidating more than 120 separate procurement actions for Anduril’s commercial solutions. “The modern battlefield is increasingly defined by software,” said Gabe Chiulli, the chief technology officer at the Department of Defense’s Office of the Chief Information Officer. “To maintain our advantage, we must be able to acquire and deploy software capabilities with speed and efficiency.”

Anduril was co-founded by Palmer Luckey, who was previously known for selling VR startup Oculus to Facebook (now Meta). Facebook fired Luckey after controversy arose following reports that he had donated to a pro-Trump political group. Luckey has insisted that the media misrepresented his political views. According to a recent feature in The New York Times, Luckey and Anduril have been embraced by the second Trump administration, thanks to his vision for remaking the U.S. military with autonomous fighter jets, drones, submarines, and more. The company, named after a magical object in “The Lord of the Rings,” brought in around $2 billion in revenue last year, the NYT reports.

Separate reports suggest that Anduril is in talks to raise a new funding round at a $60 billion valuation.

Title: Honda is killing its EVs — and any chance of competing in the future
Author: Tim De Chant
Summary: Honda has ceased its EV programs, including the development of the Acura RDX electric, Honda 0 sedan and SUV, and the GM-built Prologue. The move is attributed to U.S. tariffs and Chinese competition, but critics say Honda lacked a viable EV strategy. The decision risks falling behind in electric and software-defined vehicle advancements.
Content:
I get it; it’s not an easy time for a legacy automaker to be selling electric vehicles, what with incentives being gutted and Chinese automakers knocking at the door. But Honda is taking it to another level.

This week, Honda killed its paltry — and frankly unpromising — EV programs. What little motivation Honda had to compete in the EV arena is apparently gone, and along with it, any chance of surviving the current wave of disruption that’s sweeping the industry.

The company casts blame on U.S. tariffs and Chinese competition, two easy targets. But it never really had a viable EV strategy to begin with.

Honda kicked things off on Thursday by halting development of the electric Acura RDX and the Honda 0 sedan and SUV, three models that were the company’s first ground-up EVs — but about which very little was shared with outsiders. It continued on Friday, with Automotive News reporting that Honda was going to stop production of the Prologue, a vehicle that was essentially designed and entirely built by GM.

The decision could backfire in a number of different ways, but there are two that are most important. By shelving EVs, Honda will fall farther behind in two of the biggest shifts sweeping the automotive industry: electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles.

To Honda — and to many legacy automakers still early in the transition — an EV is just a car with a different drivetrain. Some executives may think they can wait out the awkward transition period and, when motors and batteries are fully sorted, simply swap out the fossil fuel bits. How hard could it be?

That’s a mistake, of course. Many automakers have found that dropping batteries into a car originally designed for an internal combustion engine doesn’t work out so well. It might shortcut the development cycle, but the resulting product ends up heavy, inefficient, and more costly to produce.

When developed as an original product, EVs offer automakers a chance to rethink the automobile and make it cheaper. For example, the Ford Mustang Mach E has been a sales success, but not a financial one for Ford. The Mach E is based on a heavily modified version of...

Title: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles II: Secret of the Ooze is still the most fun to be had in a TMNT movie
Author: Brian VanHooker
Summary: To celebrate the 35th anniversary of the original Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles film, Fathom and Saga Arts re-released it in theaters, initially for two days, then extended to a week due to strong presales and a $3.3 million box office. Following its success, the sequel, Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles II: The Secret of the Ooze, was also re-released, highlighting its lighter tone and nostalgic appeal.
Content:
Last August, the shellbacks made a very unexpected comeback. For the 35th anniversary of the original Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles film, Fathom and Saga Arts rereleased the superhero action-comedy in theaters. Originally, the event was supposed to run just two days, but ticket presales were so promising they extended it to a whole week and expanded the number of theaters. Then, after the film earned a surprising $3.3 million at the box office, the event was extended another week and Fathom announced that its sequel would get a similar release next year.

Now, 1991’s Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles II: The Secret of the Ooze is back in theaters, and, 35 years later, it’s still the most fun to be had with the Turtles on film, especially for those who grew up during the original Turtle-Mania of the late 1980s and early 1990s.

Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles II: The Secret of the Ooze takes place soon after the heroes defeat Shredder at the end of the first film. While they’re unable to return to their sewer home because it has been discovered by the Foot Clan, the brothers stay at the apartment of their human friend April O’Neil until they find a new place, which they eventually do in an old abandoned subway station. The Shredder, however, is not dead and begins rebuilding the Foot Clan. Worse, he creates two monstrous new mutants to seek revenge on the Turtles.

Despite being a revenge story, it’s worth noting that Secret of the Ooze was much lighter in tone than its predecessor. The 1990 film mostly adapted the original, grittier comic books, but lightened the story with humor from the 1987 cartoon series. The result was a film filled with action, drama, humor, and heart, though it was also somewhat violent, drawing complaints from parenting groups. As a result, Secret of the Ooze adopted a far lighter tone; the Turtles constantly crack jokes and take almost nothing seriously. They also rarely use their ninja weapons. Yet these changes didn’t break the Turtle formula—unlike some later films—and the movie remains beloved by fans.

The film opens with the Turtles beating up a band of burglars in a shopping mall. The tone shift is immediately apparent. Instead of attacking with swords and nunchucks, the Turtles fight with yo-yos and foam baseball bats. There’s even a scene at a deli counter where Michelangelo replaces his nunchucks with linked pepperonis, shouting, “Combat coldcuts!” while taking down the bad guys. Most of the fight scenes are playful and energetic, making them fun to watch despite their lack of seriousness.

Title: The RAM crisis could completely change the way developers make games
Author: Giovanni Colantonio
Summary: At this year's GDC, developers faced stress from layoffs, AI-related concerns, and global politics, compounded by a RAM crisis. The soaring cost of memory, driven by AI data center demands, has increased RAM prices by up to 300%, affecting both consumers and developers. Supply shortages are expected to persist for two years, impacting PC upgrades and potentially reducing game sales.
Content:
There were a lot of reasons for developers to be stressed out at this year's Game Developers Conference. Layoffs at EA the day the event started put job insecurity in the air, generative AI's presence at the show sparked existential dread, and the inescapable anxiety of world politics made it hard to focus on creating video games. Those are some heavy topics, and they were only made worse by an economic problem currently plaguing the game development industry: the RAM crisis.

The skyrocketing cost of memory driven by AI was a major topic at this year's GDC. If you didn't bring it up with someone in a conversation, it was because they did first. It was one of the most important narratives of the show, casting a shadow over Xbox's grand Project Helix ambitions and raising questions about how rising prices could impact how games are made. No developer I spoke to quite knew the next steps for navigating the crisis, but several saw it as a moment to finally have a serious conversation about the industry's insatiable hunt to create the most high-end games possible.

On the consumer side, the impact of the memory supply shortage is simple: RAM has gotten more expensive. Earlier this year, PC Gamer shared data showing that the price of RAM has seen a 300% increase in some cases. Upgrading your PC right now is pricier as a result, but the problem is only snowballing due to how many products ship with a memory component. It’s not your everyday shopper who is having trouble buying RAM, but the companies making the products that use it too.

What’s the problem? In very simplified terms, AI data centers require a lot of memory. There are only a handful of major memory manufacturers (Samsung and Micron, for instance) making those products, and they can make a lot more money if they sell specialized parts to AI companies rather than consumers. With more chips spoken for, low supply has led to higher costs for the rest of us. You’d think that the easy solution would be “just produce more chips,” but that’s easier said than done for a variety of factors, including the fact that RAM requires a lot of rare metals to produce. It’s also not as simple as spinning up some competing manufacturers, as that would take years. We’re at a dead end, and developers at GDC told me that we might be there for another two years.

The developers I spoke to were split on just how much that would impact game development directly. Some said that they were annoyed that it was more expensive to upgrade their own personal PCs, but that they didn’t feel it was having a knock-on effect for creating their games. Others in the space noted some tangential side effects.

“It does wind up in our conversations particularly when we’re looking at the competition,” one marketing professional told me. “If you’re asking people to suddenly pay more for their systems, that’s fewer games than they can purchase. So it does change some of the conversations around how we have to adapt.”

Title: Invincible's showrunners reveal the brutal truth about 'superhero fatigue'
Author: Jake Kleinman
Summary: Superhero movies and shows are experiencing a decline in popularity, with audiences demanding more innovation as familiarity grows. Despite this, creators like Robert Kirkman of Invincible believe the genre remains strong but must evolve to stand out amid increased competition from streaming content and classic TV shows. Success now requires originality and high-quality execution across platforms.
Content:
Superhero movies aren’t the cultural force they once were, with both Hollywood executives and fans noting a decline in their blockbuster appeal. While Marvel’s dominance was once unmatched and even mediocre DC films could perform well at the box office, the returns on comic-book-based action films are diminishing. Television has also seen a dip in enthusiasm—reactions to shows like Wonder Man compared to WandaVision highlight this trend, with the latter earning a sequel that was essentially movie-sized.

Are onscreen superheroes past their prime? Not according to the showrunners of Invincible, which returns for its fourth season on Prime Video on March 18. Franchise creator Robert Kirkman told Polygon that the superhero obsession remains strong, but audiences have become more savvy and accustomed to superhero stories. The issue isn’t boredom but the need for the genre to evolve. Being merely competent is no longer enough, as audiences have grown used to the storytelling style established by Marvel, which releases multiple films annually.

Kirkman explained that superhero stories now need to elevate the genre, offering something new or more refined to stand out. His co-showrunner, Simon Racioppa, agreed, emphasizing that the bar for success has risen. To truly stand out, a show must be unique, incorporate fresh elements, or execute exceptionally well across all aspects.

The challenge extends beyond superheroes. When the Marvel Cinematic Universe launched with Iron Man in 2008, streaming services like Netflix were just emerging. At that time, movies competed mainly with other theatrical releases. Now, each new superhero film or show must contend with a vast array of content—every TV show, movie, and video game ever made. Kirkman pointed out that on streaming platforms, a show is competing not just with current productions but with classics like Friends and Seinfeld, making it harder to break through.

He concluded that the current media landscape demands creators to find ways to be distinctive and capture audience attention amid endless options, whether superhero-themed or not.

Title: Reminders of Him
Author: Unknown
Summary: "Reminders of Him" (2026) is a PG-13 romance drama directed by Vanessa Caswill, set for release on March 13, 2026. Starring Maika Monroe and others, it follows Kenna, who after a mistake leads to prison, and seven years later seeks redemption and reconciliation. The film is produced by Universal Pictures.
Content:
Reminders of Him (2026) is a romance drama rated PG-13, with a runtime of 114 minutes. It is set to be released on March 13, 2026, directed by Vanessa Caswill and written by Colleen Hoover and Lauren Levine. The cast includes Maika Monroe, Tyriq Withers, Rudy Pankow, Lainey Wilson, Lauren Graham, and Bradley Whitford. The film is produced by Universal Pictures.

The story explores themes of motherhood, forgiveness, and love overcoming mistakes. After a perfect outing with her boyfriend, Kenna (Maika Monroe) makes an unbearable mistake that results in her imprisonment. Seven years later, she seeks redemption and reconciliation.

Title: ‘Pretty Lethal’ Review: A Ballet Action Romp With Limited Imagination
Author: Siddhant Adlakha
Summary: "Pretty Lethal," directed by Vicky Jewson, follows a talented ballerina, Bones (Maddie Ziegler), in a Central European mob setting. The film features dance and action elements but suffers from a predictable premise, limited character development, and underused ensemble members. It combines dance and crime tropes with mixed success, ultimately offering diminishing entertainment value.
Content:
What if an American ballerina quintet was dropped into a mob flick in Central Europe? Director Vicky Jewson answers this question in “Pretty Lethal,” at first with aplomb, but eventually with all the follies this setting implies, and few of the imaginative strengths. The film does a small handful of things rather well, so it does each them more than once, from repeated action beats, to popular needle drops about dancing (“Rhythm is a Dancer,” “Everybody Dance Now”) whether or not they tonally fit. But its premise never evolves enough to let loose in ridiculous fashion, yielding diminishing returns.

Bones (Maddie Ziegler) is a street-smart, rough-around-the-edges dancer in a world of wealth, but she’s made it far enough to earn a major solo, making her a target of ire for her spoiled, prim-and-proper ballet company teammate Princess (Lana Condor). Their very names evoke the scrappy and the bratty, broad characteristics that make them a halfway decent fit for the forthcoming action rendezvous. When we first meet their ballet company — led by their conscientious British instructor Thorna (Lydia Leonard) — their variation is discordant, owing to Princess’ pretty jealousies. But, after aggressively shoulder-checking Bones, the group comes together for a well-coordinated performance in rehearsal, proving they can get along if they need to.

Unfortunately, the movie’s problems start here, well before their trip to Hungary for a major competition. For one thing, while the dancers performing in perfect unison is a logistical necessity for their being invited to a grand stage, having them prove their coordination this early robs them of a more cinematic alternative, i.e., having to come together as a unit the more the film goes on. For another, the other three teammates are rendered practically invisible, despite being a key part of the movie’s ensemble. There’s Grace (Avantika), whose entire personality is her comic piety, though she’s eventually afforded some physical comedy by way of accidental drug ingestion. But even this caricature is more than what’s afforded to Zoe (Iris Apatow) and her Deaf sister Chloe (Millicent Simmonds), whose collective function is the former interp.

Title: Paramount Passes on ‘G.I. Joe’ Treatment from Max Landis (EXCLUSIVE)
Author: Varietymattdonnelly
Summary: Paramount Pictures and Hasbro are not proceeding with Max Landis's G.I. Joe screenplay, citing creative differences. The studio is exploring other scripts, including one from Danny McBride. Landis expressed gratitude for the opportunity, describing his pitch as a subversive idea about Cobra’s takeover. His career faced controversy in 2016 due to misconduct allegations.
Content:
Paramount Pictures and Hasbro are not moving forward with a take on a new G.I. Joe movie from screenwriter Max Landis, multiple sources told Variety. Landis, son of “Trading Places” director John Landis, was engaged by the studio and the toymaker earlier this year for a script treatment surrounding the iconic action figures. New film leadership at Paramount are prioritizing the relaunch of this IP and others, including “Star Trek.” 

Paramount passed on Landis’s treatment for creative reasons, one source added. The studio is still in talks with and awaiting scripts from other writers, including “The Righteous Gemstones” star Danny McBride. 

Reached via Instagram, Landis told Variety he was “tremendously grateful to have been given the opportunity.” He elaborated that his pitch came together “very serendipitously based on the coolness of the weird and subversive idea.” That idea centered around the film franchise’s villain, Cobra, “having successfully taken over the world and reduced G.I. Joe to a conspiracy theory. But this is just how big IP development always is. Honestly, I was surprised it was reported at all.” 

Paramount Pictures did not immediately respond to a request for comment. A representative for Hasbro could not immediately be reached for comment. 

News of Landis pitching on the project grabbed headlines, as his career cratered after allegations of sexual assault and other misconduct were brought against him in 2016 by eight women. At the time, Landis was dropped from agency CAA and multiple development projects set up at studios were canceled.

Title: ‘Manhood’ Review: Daniel Lombroso Sensitively Navigates Insecurities of American Masculinity While Breaking Its Taboos
Author: Tomilaffly
Summary: Daniel Lombroso’s documentary “Manhood” examines male insecurities around penis size, highlighting the growing sexual wellness industry. It follows three men, including Bill Moore, a pioneer in penile enhancement procedures, who aims to normalize such treatments. The film sensitively explores societal pressures on men, balancing humor and serious reflection on masculinity and self-worth.
Content:
While society’s unrealistic expectations for women and the impossible physical standards we are held up against are often discussed and critiqued, the similar pressures men face aren’t as widely talked about in culture. That’s understandable, since the misogynistic toxicity women deal with has age-old ramifications. But that’s not to say that men are spared from judgment, especially in the narcissistic Instagram era, that can directly affect one’s inner self-worth and public treatment. A topic Celine Song’s “Materialists” smartly addressed by mentioning the expensive limb-lengthening surgeries men opt for. 

Among the most taboo — and yet, among the most common — of male insecurities, Daniel Lombroso’s “Manhood” explores another industry that promises a girthier sense of male pride: increasing the size of one’s member. On the page, it might seem an impossible ask from a movie to walk a fine line when discussing the male obsession over penis size—how to balance a gentle approach with serious contemplation and humor. Thankfully, Lombroso does so with ease, following the journeys of three different men, each with intriguing stories that the filmmaker navigates sensitively.

The first is Dallas entrepreneur Bill Moore, a trailblazer in the penile enhancement field, a core part of the growing sexual wellness industry. Operating out of a Texan strip mall in his early days and now running nationwide practices with board-certified urologists, Moore attempts to make penis enlargement via impermanent injections as commonplace as Botox. The Kafka quote that opens the film, “I was ashamed of myself when I realized life was a costume party and I attended with my real face,” reflects the philosophical core of Moore’s business. He understands the unspoken shame men can carry within themselves and seeks to do something productive about it.

Title: Staff complain that xAI is flailing because of constant upheaval
Author: Stephen Morris and Cristina Criddle, Financial Times
Summary: Elon Musk ordered job cuts at x AI amid poor performance of its coding tools, leading to the departure of several cofounders and staff. Musk has brought in SpaceX and Tesla managers to audit the startup, which is being rebuilt from scratch. The company struggles to compete with Anthropic and OpenAI, with efforts focused on improving data quality and AI products.
Content:
Elon Musk has ordered another round of job cuts at x AI after growing frustrated with the poor performance of its coding product, forcing out several more cofounders and bringing in “fixers” from SpaceX and Tesla to audit the startup. The latest overhaul of the two-year-old startup follows the success of Anthropic and OpenAI, whose AI coding tools have shaken up the software industry, according to multiple sources familiar with the decisions.

Musk has increased pressure after merging SpaceX with x AI in a $1.25 billion deal, as he aims to meet a June deadline for what could be the largest stock market listing in history. His goals include launching AI data centers into space, building factories on the Moon, and colonizing Mars. Despite relentless efforts to catch up with rivals, x AI’s Grok chatbot and coding product have failed to gain significant traction with paying users or businesses.

“x AI was not built right the first time around, so it is being rebuilt from the foundations up,” Musk posted on X on Thursday. “Same thing happened with Tesla.” SpaceX and Musk did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

Managers from SpaceX and Tesla have been seconded to review x AI employees’ work and have dismissed some after deeming their efforts inadequate, according to two people with direct knowledge. A key focus has been the quality of data used to train the models, which is a major reason why its coding product lags behind competitors like Anthropic’s Claude Code and OpenAI’s Codex.

The review has led to the departure of two more cofounders. Zihang Dai, a senior member of the technical staff who had publicly acknowledged that x AI was behind on coding, left this week. Guodong Zhang, who managed pre-training of Grok models and was blamed for issues with the coding product, also left after being relieved of his duties by Musk. He confirmed on X that Thursday was his last day.

Following these departures, only Manuel Kroiss and Ross Nordeen remain of the original 11 cofounders who helped Musk establish x AI in San Francisco in March 2023. Last month, Musk criticized the coding team for falling behind in a town hall meeting posted online and announced a reorganization after several other cofounders, including Greg Yang, Tony Wu, and Jimmy Ba, had been removed.

Toby Pohlen, a former DeepMind researcher, was appointed to lead the “Macrohard” project to develop digital agents capable of replicating entire software companies, which Musk called the “most important” initiative. The project’s name is a humorous reference to Microsoft. However, Pohlen left the company 16 days later. Musk has since redeployed Ashok Elluswamy, head of AI software at Tesla, to reboot the Macrohard effort.

Title: NASA officials sidestepped questions on Artemis II risks—there's a reason why
Author: Stephen Clark
Summary: NASA officials acknowledged the high risks of the Artemis II Moon mission, the first crewed lunar flight since 1972, due to its unprecedented distance and limited testing data. Despite uncertainties, NASA confirmed readiness to proceed with launch scheduled for April 1, emphasizing the mission's exploratory nature and ongoing risk mitigation efforts.
Content:
When talking about risk during a press conference on Thursday, the NASA officials in charge of the upcoming Artemis II Moon mission hedged their answers. Reporters’ questions on the risks were certainly valid and appropriate. In an open society, it is vital to set expectations for any hazardous venture such as spaceflight—most importantly for the astronauts actually making the journey, but also for NASA’s workforce, the White House, lawmakers, and members of the public paying for the endeavor.

What’s more, Artemis II will be the first mission since 1972 to fly humans to the vicinity of the Moon. This is not following the well-trodden yet perilous path that astronauts take to reach the International Space Station, just a few hundred miles above Earth. Artemis II will travel more than 1,000 times farther from Earth than the ISS, departing on a trajectory taking the mission several thousand miles beyond the far side of the Moon. The mission will last nine days from liftoff in Florida to splashdown in the Pacific Ocean. The four-person crew will ride a rocket and spacecraft—the Space Launch System and Orion—that have flown together just once before. The sheer novelty of the mission makes it difficult to quantify the risk, NASA officials said Thursday.

With just a single data point from flight testing—the unpiloted Artemis I demo mission in 2022—NASA managers were reluctant to publicize the bottom-line number from the probabilistic risk assessment for Artemis II. Lori Glaze, NASA’s acting associate administrator for exploration system development, said the agency completed an assessment for Artemis II, but questioned the exercise’s usefulness. “I think sometimes we get tricked into believing that those numbers are somehow really telling us something critically important,” Glaze said. “I think they’re valuable. I think we can do things in a relative sense to measure what’s more risky or less risky.”

Glaze and other members of the Artemis II management team were speaking with reporters at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center following a flight readiness review. The two-day conclave in Florida provided the forum for a “very open” and “transparent” discussion of NASA’s “risk posture” heading into the Artemis II launch, and “how we’re mitigating those risks,” Glaze said. The decision-makers present for the meeting unanimously agreed to continue final preparations for the Artemis II mission, now scheduled for liftoff no earlier than April 1 at 6:24 pm EDT (22:24 UTC). “It is a test flight, and it is not without risk, but our team and our hardware are ready,” Glaze said. The four astronauts training to fly on Artemis II joined the Flight Readiness Review (FRR) virtually from their home base in Houston. Their participation included discussion of the Orion spacecraft’s heat shield and reentry trajectory.

Title: Woman sneezes out maggots after fly larvae get trapped in her deviated septum
Author: Beth Mole
Summary: A 58-year-old woman in Greece holds the record for the longest parasitic sheep bot fly in her nose, with larvae reaching the pupal stage—a first in human cases. She was exposed to flies near sheep grazing fields, leading to symptoms and the eventual removal of multiple larvae, including a pupa. Experts previously believed fly development in humans stopped at the first larval stage.
Content:
A 58-year-old woman in Greece appears to hold the record for growing a parasitic sheep bot fly in her nose the longest, almost creating a snot rocket that could literally fly. Usually, when the sheep bot fly accidentally enters a human’s nose, the first-stage larvae they deliver do not develop further. In its normal target—a sheep’s nose—the larvae would move into the sinuses, feed, grow, and molt into second- and third-stage larvae. From there, the flies (Oestrus ovis) drip from the nose onto the ground, burrow into the soil, pupate, and emerge as adult flies.

For a long time, experts believed that the flies could not complete their development in humans beyond the first larval stage. However, a few human cases involving second- and third-stage larvae have been reported in recent decades. The woman’s case, reported in the Journal of Emerging Infectious Diseases by a medical entomologist and colleagues, goes the furthest yet, with findings of pupa and a puparium—the hard casing of a pupa—in her nose.

The experts noted that the woman worked outdoors on a Greek island near a field with grazing sheep. On a hot, dry September day, she recalled a swarm of flies bombarding her face. About a week later, she experienced facial pain and developed a cough over the next two to three weeks. These were her only symptoms until October 15, when she sneezed and reported that “worms” came out of her nose. They were, in fact, late-stage sheep bot fly larvae.

She underwent surgery to remove the larvae, which recovered 10 specimens at various stages, including a pupa. Genetic testing and DNA sequencing confirmed they were sheep bot flies, as did visual inspection of two third-stage larvae and the puparium. The third instar larva was yellowish with rows of spines on the ventral surface, and the broken puparium was black and wrinkled, containing remnants of the pupa.

Not only had experts never found a pupa in a human nose before, but they also believed that development to that stage was “biologically implausible.” They noted that the paranasal sinus environment does not meet the temperature and humidity requirements for pupation, and host secretions and immune responses further complicate the development process.
